interpretations.interpretation: A “servant” figure who suffers on behalf of the people: the audience could understand the verse as describing a righteous agent (possibly a leader/representative) whose pain, low status, and social rejection come because others refuse to recognize God’s purpose in him.

Israel as a suffering collective: “a man of sorrows” might be heard metaphorically as the nation in exile or under oppression—so the “hiding of faces” would reflect how surrounding communities and even fellow Israelites react to the humiliation of Israel.

A specific righteous prophet or holy person: listeners might take the imagery (“despised,” “not esteemed,” faces hidden) as the experience of a faithful individual who speaks God’s word and is rejected, despite being characterized by grief and suffering.

A critique of communal misrecognition: the line “we did not think/consider him” could be read as an accusation toward the audience’s own community—showing they misjudged a suffering, lowly person and therefore failed to interpret events through God’s perspective.

An expected theme of divine reversal: the description of someone socially “בזוי” (worthless in public eyes) could signal to the audience that God’s saving action will come through the lowly, overturning ordinary human standards of honor and status.

An account of how suffering produces shame and avoidance: the audience could read “like one from whom people hide their faces” as reflecting the social and psychological reality of ostracism—people distancing themselves from visible grief, illness, or hardship rather than offering compassion.
